text.title: dbt Transformation Conventions and Data Quality
text.summary: dbt model naming, materialisation choices, data quality tool selection (Great Expectations/Soda/dbt tests), data contracts, and error handling (DLQ, idempotency, retry strategies).

section.title: dbt Model Conventions

rule.statement: Follow the stg_ / int_ / fct_ / dim_ naming convention and never reference raw source tables directly from intermediate or mart models.
rule.rationale: Bypassing staging models means business logic depends on raw source schema — any upstream schema change breaks downstream models. Staging models create a stable abstraction layer.

list.item: stg_<source>__<entity>.sql: one model per source table, light transforms only (rename, cast, dedupe). Materialise as view or ephemeral.
int_<verb>_<entity>.sql: business logic groupings. Use verbs: int_orders_joined, int_users_aggregated. Materialise as view or ephemeral.
fct_<entity>.sql: fact tables, append-only events. Materialise as table or incremental.
dim_<entity>.sql: dimension tables, frequently accessed. Materialise as table.

rule.statement: Use incremental materialisation for large fact tables; always define the unique_key and on_schema_change strategy.
rule.rationale: Full-table refresh of a 1B-row fact table takes hours and wastes warehouse compute. Incremental processes only new/changed rows. Without unique_key, incremental runs can produce duplicates.

section.reference: Canonical staging model with source() reference, type casting, and row_number() deduplication

rule.statement: Apply dbt tests at the transformation layer for structural integrity (not_null, unique, relationships); use Great Expectations or Soda for business rule validation at ingestion boundaries.
rule.rationale: dbt tests run after transformation and catch model logic errors. Great Expectations / Soda run at ingestion and catch upstream source quality issues before they reach the warehouse.

list.item: dbt tests: not_null/unique on keys, accepted_values for enums, relationships for foreign keys. Integrated with dbt run.
Great Expectations: Python-first, 300+ expectations, data docs generation, complex compliance validation.
Soda: YAML-first (SodaCL), accessible for non-engineers, continuous monitoring with anomaly detection alerts.

rule.statement: Route failed records to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) rather than discarding them or halting the pipeline.
rule.rationale: Discarding failed records causes silent data loss. Halting the pipeline blocks all downstream consumers. DLQ preserves the failed records for manual review and reprocessing without blocking the healthy path.

list.item: DLQ message: include original topic, partition, offset, timestamp, original key/value, error time, retry count.
Retry strategy: exponential backoff for transient failures (network, API limits); fixed delay for known recovery times; circuit breaker for cascade prevention.
Idempotency check: before processing, check if record_id was already successfully processed; skip duplicates.

antipattern.description: Silently dropping records that fail schema validation or business rule checks.
antipattern.reason: Silent drops make data quality failures invisible. The pipeline reports 100% success while data completeness degrades, which only surfaces when a report is wrong.
